title: The Canterbury Tales. The Cook's Tale. by Geoffrey Chaucer — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
THE PROLOGUE.

THE Cook of London, while the Reeve thus spake,
For joy he laugh'd and clapp'd him on the back:
"Aha!" quoth he, "for Christes passion,
This Miller had a sharp conclusion,
Upon this argument of herbergage.
Well saide Solomon in his language,
meaning: "The Canterbury Tales. The Cook's Tale." by Geoffrey Chaucer is a sad and love and deep poem consisting of 105 lines. The poet explores themes of sad and love and deep through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "THE PROLOGUE. ...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Geoffrey Chaucer's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
